The Startup Ideas Podcast (SIP)
@startupideaspod
The AI stack behind my viral content (10M+ views)
Learn the exact AI workflow behind Cova's viral Instagram Reels. FreePick for set design, Manus for creative research, Claude Code for project planning; all from a dorm room.
Cova gets millions of views on her Instagram Reels. She's worked with NVIDIA and Adobe. They call her when they want short-form video that goes viral.
Here's what most people don't know: she films in her bedroom.
No Hollywood set. No studio budget. Just a phone, a handful of AI tools, and a system that makes every frame look intentional.
In this guide, she shares her entire workflow for the first time: how she uses Manus to reverse-engineer what works, FreePick to transform any background, and Obsidian + Claude Code to plan and scale her content like a system — not a guessing game.

Step 01: Use Manus to Reverse-Engineer Any Creator's Style

Most creators watch a video they like and try to copy the vibe from memory. Cova feeds the video to Manus and gets a full breakdown.
Her prompt approach: tell Manus you like the style and story of a specific video, then ask it to break it down in terms of style and aesthetic keywords, script transcription separated into story sections, and an overall plan for how to replicate it.
Manus doesn't just summarize. It runs scripts to parse the video, extract the transcription, and analyze frame by frame. Other tools like Claude or ChatGPT tend to make assumptions. Manus does the work granularly.
When Cova tested it on one of her own Tamagotchi Reels, the output nailed her process: five-act story structure, typography system broken into titles, section headers, and captions, and even her aesthetic keywords — "dark academia maker, cozy hacker den, bedroom devlog."
She puts the burden of proof on the AI. She doesn't tell it what to find. She lets it come back with a plan.
The real move: don't just analyze one creator. Run this on 5–10 creators you admire. Pull what you like from each — one creator's nostalgic vibe, another's storytelling structure, someone else's music choices. Every creator is a mashup of different aesthetics. This process helps you find yours.
"Every creator is kind of a mashup of different kinds of creatures and different kinds of aesthetics they like." — Cova

Step 02: Transform Any Background With FreePick

This is where most creators lose. The room looks bare. The lighting is flat. Retention drops because there's nothing interesting to look at.
Cova's fix: take a single frame from your talking-head footage, bring it into FreePick's image editor, and use Nano Banana Pro to generate set additions.
Her workflow is dead simple. Open the visual feature in FreePick so you can annotate directly on the image. Circle or mark the area you want to change. Prompt with something short — "more flowers" or "orange tulips in a vase." She deliberately keeps prompts general because simpler prompts let the model lean into what it's actually good at rather than fighting a hyper-specific instruction.
She showed a before-and-after from a dorm room shoot: the original had a bare wall with nothing on it. After FreePick, she'd added fairy lights, a window, a vinyl record player, bookshelves, a fan. Same room. Completely different video.
Then she takes the generated image back into Adobe Premiere (or CapCut if you want the simpler route) and masks the new elements onto the original footage. Draw a mask around the generation, overlay it on your talking-head clip, and you're done.
One caveat: make sure the AI-generated objects aren't overlapping your body too closely. If your body moves and the mask doesn't track, it looks fake. Prompt again and ask for the object to be placed farther from the subject.
This isn't just about aesthetics. Better backgrounds mean higher retention. Higher retention means Instagram pushes your video to new audiences. More views, more followers, more leverage.
"I would not be a creator if I did not use AI." — Cova

Step 03: Create Impossible Transitions With AI Video Gen

Cova showed a transition where she appears as a kid in a photo that comes to life, then cuts to her present-day self saying "I'm an artist." That shot is impossible to film. She made it in minutes.
The process: take your start frame and end frame. Bring the start frame into FreePick's video generator. She uses Seedance Pro because it generates audio too — you get the kid laughing, ambient sounds, all baked in.
For prompting video gen, the key is describing camera movement first and narrating the scene like a story. "Camera is static. On the table is a picture of a kid. The kid is waving their arms gleefully."
One critical rule: avoid negative phrasing. Don't say "the photo doesn't move." Models don't process negation well — they latch onto keywords regardless of word order. Say "camera is stationary" instead. Positive terminology gets better results.
FreePick also has a prompt editor that optimizes your prompt for the model. Cova says it improves output almost every time.
She stitches these 3–4 second AI-generated clips between her real footage. The first 15 seconds of a video are everything. These transitions create the aha moment that makes people stay.

Step 04: Plan Everything in Obsidian + Claude Code

Cova keeps every project in Obsidian — scripts, storyboard templates, style guides, editor briefs.
She built her own storyboard template that tracks every element needed for a video. When she finishes a script, she opens her Obsidian vault in Cursor or Claude Code and tells it to turn the script into the storyboard template. Within a minute, it converts a raw script into a structured table she can review and hand off.
She also keeps a personal style guide in Obsidian — her "Cova-cut style guide" — so when Claude does a first pass on a storyboard, it follows her creative rules.
The system loops together: use Manus to generate a style guide from creators you admire. Export it to Obsidian. Edit it to match your taste. Now tools like Cursor and Claude Code can access it whenever you build a new project.
Her advice for getting started: create templates. If you have a way of doing things, document it. In order to scale content, you always need to build great systems. AI executes the systems you don't want to do manually — but only if you've documented them first.

The Full Tool Stack

Video editing: Adobe Premiere Pro + After Effects for VFX
Image generation: Nano Banana Pro (via FreePick)
Video generation: Seedance Pro, Kling 3
Creative research: Manus
Project planning: Obsidian + Claude Code
Task management: Poke

Final Thoughts

Cova built her entire visual brand from a dorm room. No studio, no production team, no gate.
The tools are all available right now. FreePick, Manus, Claude Code, Premiere, Obsidian. The workflow is learnable in an afternoon.
The creators who win from here aren't the ones with the biggest budgets. They're the ones who are smart about which tools they use and how they combine them. Differentiation is the game now. The tech is the same for everyone. The creative system you build around it is what sets you apart.
"There is no better time than now to make art — so you should go and do that." — Cova
